stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing an 18-month-old boy whose mother is worried about three episodes of him going limp and turning blue around his lips for around 20 seconds after he was scolded or denied something he wanted. Each episode ended with rapid recovery, no post-event drowsiness, no incontinence and no jerking movements observed. He is meeting all developmental milestones and is otherwise well. There is no family history of sudden death, syncope or seizures.

data_table.Examination: Alert and playful; no dysmorphic features; cardiovascular and neurological examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: FBC Hb 118, MCV 78, ferritin 24 microg/L; ECG sinus rhythm with QTc 410 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassure parents about the benign self-limiting nature of cyanotic breath-holding spells and provide simple first-aid safety advice
options.rationale: Cyanotic breath-holding spells are a benign reflex phenomenon peaking at 6 to 18 months with normal investigations; reassurance and basic safety advice are the primary management. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent life-threatening event)

options.text: Commence sodium valproate 10 mg/kg/day in two divided doses with serum drug-level monitoring every three months in clinic
options.rationale: Anticonvulsants are not indicated for breath-holding spells which are reflex syncope not epileptic seizures; sodium valproate carries teratogenic and hepatotoxic risks unwarranted here. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent life-threatening event)

options.text: Arrange outpatient sleep-deprived EEG and 24-hour ambulatory video-EEG monitoring to exclude an underlying focal epilepsy syndrome
options.rationale: EEG is reserved for episodes with seizure features (jerking, prolonged unresponsiveness, post-ictal phase) which are absent here; routine EEG yields false positives in normal toddlers. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent life-threatening event)

options.text: Refer to paediatric cardiology for 24-hour Holter monitoring and exercise stress echocardiogram to exclude long QT syndrome
options.rationale: Cardiac referral is reserved for syncope with exertional or postural triggers, abnormal ECG or family history of sudden death; reflex triggers with normal ECG do not warrant escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent life-threatening event)

options.text: Commence oral ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg/day elemental iron for empirical supplementation despite ferritin within the normal reference range
options.rationale: Iron supplementation reduces spell frequency only when iron deficiency is present; this child has normal ferritin and supplementation risks constipation without benefit. (Source: RCH CPG: Apparent life-threatening event)

explanation.summary: Cyanotic breath-holding spells are reflex anoxic episodes triggered by frustration, anger or pain in toddlers aged 6 months to 4 years. The child cries, holds breath in expiration, becomes cyanotic and may lose consciousness for under 60 seconds with rapid full recovery. Distinguishing features from seizures include the consistent emotional trigger, absence of post-ictal phase and absence of stereotyped jerking. Iron deficiency anaemia worsens frequency, so checking ferritin is reasonable; supplementation only helps when deficiency is present. Reassurance with simple first-aid (lay child on side, avoid mouth interference, reinforce that spells are not harmful) is the cornerstone.
key_points: Reflex syncope triggered by emotion, not epilepsy
Check ferritin; treat only if deficient
EEG reserved for atypical features
Cardiac workup only if exertional trigger or family sudden death
explanation.pearls: Resolution by age 5 years occurs in over 90 percent; persistence beyond 6 years or new exertional triggers warrant cardiology review for long QT or catecholaminergic polymorphic VT.

stem: You are the GP shared-care obstetric registrar reviewing a 32-year-old primigravida at 12+3 weeks gestation. She has had her first trimester combined screen at her chosen radiology clinic and brings the report. The fetus has appropriate crown-rump length for dates. She has no chronic medical conditions, no family history of chromosomal abnormality and is in a low-risk demographic for aneuploidy. She is keen to know what monitoring she requires for the remainder of pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Fundus not yet palpable abdominally; no clinical concerns
data_table.Investigations: Nuchal translucency 1.6 mm, PAPP-A 1.0 MoM, free beta-hCG 1.1 MoM; calculated risk trisomy 21 1 in 8500, trisomy 18 1 in 25000
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin with folate 500 micrograms and iodine

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her antenatal care?

options.text: Order non-invasive prenatal testing (cell-free DNA) because the combined screen alone is insufficient to exclude common trisomies in a low-risk woman
options.rationale: NIPT is offered as an alternative or follow-up to combined screening but is not mandated after a normal low-risk combined screen result; routine NIPT after a reassuring screen is not standard. (Source: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Refer for diagnostic amniocentesis at 16 weeks gestation to provide karyotype confirmation despite the reassuring screening risk estimate
options.rationale: Amniocentesis carries a 1 in 200 procedure-related miscarriage risk and is reserved for high-risk screening, known parental rearrangement or maternal anxiety after counselling; routine use is not indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Continue routine antenatal care and arrange the second trimester morphology ultrasound between 18 and 22 weeks gestation as scheduled
options.rationale: A low-risk combined screen result allows continuation of routine antenatal care; the 18 to 22 week morphology scan remains the next scheduled imaging milestone for structural assessment. (Source: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Arrange a fetal detailed cardiac scan at 14 weeks gestation regardless of normal nuchal translucency to screen for congenital cardiac anomalies
options.rationale: Early fetal echocardiography is reserved for increased nuchal translucency, maternal diabetes, family history or teratogen exposure; isolated low-risk pregnancy does not warrant early cardiac imaging. (Source: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

options.text: Repeat the nuchal translucency scan at 13+6 weeks gestation to confirm the initial measurement before continuing routine antenatal care
options.rationale: Combined screening is performed once at 11+0 to 13+6 weeks; a reassuring result does not require repeat measurement and the window closes at 13+6 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

explanation.summary: First trimester combined screening at 11+0 to 13+6 weeks combines nuchal translucency, maternal serum PAPP-A and free beta-hCG with maternal age to estimate trisomy 21, 18 and 13 risks. A risk below 1 in 1000 is conventionally classified low-risk and allows continuation of routine antenatal care. The 18 to 22 week morphology ultrasound remains the next scheduled imaging milestone, regardless of combined screen result. NIPT is an alternative or follow-up offered to women who want higher detection rates; diagnostic testing is reserved for high-risk results, known parental abnormalities or after careful counselling for maternal request.
key_points: Combined screen risk under 1 in 1000 is low-risk
Morphology scan at 18 to 22 weeks remains next scheduled imaging
NIPT is optional after low-risk screen, not mandated
Diagnostic invasive testing reserved for high-risk screening
explanation.pearls: An NT above 3.5 mm with normal karyotype still confers a 5 to 10 percent residual risk of congenital cardiac anomaly and warrants a detailed fetal echocardiogram at 20 weeks.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Prenatal screening and diagnostic testing

subtopic_detail: Combined hormonal contraception eligibility with migraine
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 22-year-old nulligravid woman who requests the combined oral contraceptive pill for cycle regulation and contraception. She reports migraine headaches approximately once a month with no visual or sensory aura, lasting under a day and managed with paracetamol. She does not smoke, has BMI 24, BP 118/72, and no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism, stroke, ischaemic heart disease or breast cancer. She has read online that all migraine sufferers must avoid the pill and would like clarification.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; no focal neurology; no breast lumps; thyroid normal
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening up to date; STI screen negative; not pregnant
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol PRN for migraine; nil regular

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refuse combined hormonal contraception and prescribe progestogen-only options because any migraine history places her in WHO category 4 absolute contraindication
options.rationale: WHO category 4 applies to migraine with aura at any age; migraine without aura under 35 is only category 2 and not an absolute contraindication. (Source: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC

options.text: Recommend depot medroxyprogesterone acetate intramuscular injection every 12 weeks as the only safe option for any woman with a migraine history
options.rationale: DMPA is an option but is not the only safe choice; combined methods remain acceptable for migraine without aura under 35 after counselling on warning symptoms. (Source: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC

options.text: Insert a copper intrauterine device today as the only reliable contraception that avoids any hormonal influence on migraine frequency or stroke risk
options.rationale: Copper IUD is acceptable but is not the only option; combined hormonal contraception remains a valid choice for migraine without aura under 35 after appropriate counselling. (Source: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC

options.text: Decline to prescribe combined contraception and recommend barrier methods alone with neurology referral before any hormonal contraceptive can be considered
options.rationale: Neurology referral is not required to prescribe combined contraception for migraine without aura; the consultation can be completed in primary care with appropriate counselling. (Source: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC

options.text: Prescribe the combined oral contraceptive pill and counsel her to stop immediately and seek review if she develops any new aura, focal neurology or worsening headache
options.rationale: Migraine without aura under age 35 is WHO MEC category 2 for combined hormonal contraception; benefits generally outweigh risks with explicit advice to stop if aura emerges. (Source: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC)
source_refs: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC

explanation.summary: Migraine eligibility for combined hormonal contraception depends on aura status and age. Migraine without aura at any age is WHO MEC category 2 (advantages generally outweigh theoretical risks); migraine with aura at any age is category 4 (unacceptable risk) because aura confers an independent ischaemic stroke risk that compounds with combined hormonal contraception. The safest practice is to prescribe combined contraception for migraine without aura under 35 with explicit advice to stop immediately if any aura, focal neurology or worsening headache develops. Aura is defined as gradual development of unilateral fully reversible neurological symptoms over 5 to 60 minutes preceding the headache.
key_points: Migraine without aura under 35: WHO MEC 2 for CHC
Migraine with aura at any age: WHO MEC 4 absolute contraindication
Counsel to stop CHC immediately if aura develops
Aura is fully reversible neurology lasting 5 to 60 minutes
explanation.pearls: An ischaemic stroke odds ratio of approximately 6 for women with migraine with aura on combined hormonal contraception underpins the WHO MEC 4 classification.

sources.label: Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Healthcare: UK MEC

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 38-year-old para 2 woman whose menstrual bleeding has been heavy for the past 18 months, soaking through a super pad every two hours on her three heaviest days and passing thumb-sized clots. She has regular 28-day cycles, no intermenstrual bleeding and no post-coital bleeding. She has completed her family and her cervical screening is current. Pelvic ultrasound shows a normal uterus with a thin regular endometrium and normal ovaries.

data_table.Examination: No conjunctival pallor; abdomen soft and non-tender; bimanual unremarkable; cervix healthy
data_table.Investigations: Hb 105 g/L, MCV 78, ferritin 8 microg/L, TSH 1.8; pelvic ultrasound normal; cervical screening current

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Prescribe tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily during menses only with oral iron and review the response to monotherapy in three months
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid alone reduces blood loss by 30 to 50 percent but is less effective than the LNG-IUS which is the first-line option for ovulatory heavy menstrual bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Insert a levonorgestrel intrauterine system today and commence oral iron replacement with three-month review of bleeding and ferritin response
options.rationale: Levonorgestrel-releasing IUS is the first-line treatment for ovulatory heavy menstrual bleeding with normal uterine cavity; it reduces blood loss by around 90 percent and concurrent iron treats the deficiency. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Refer for endometrial ablation as first-line definitive management given completed family and a uterus with no submucosal pathology on imaging
options.rationale: Endometrial ablation is reserved for women who have failed or declined medical management including the LNG-IUS, not as first-line definitive treatment. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Arrange outpatient hysteroscopy with endometrial biopsy as the next step before any medical or device-based therapy can be safely commenced
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy and biopsy are reserved for intermenstrual or postmenopausal bleeding, suspicious ultrasound or treatment failure; normal pelvic ultrasound in a 38-year-old does not mandate it. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

options.text: Prescribe combined oral contraceptive pill for cycle and bleeding control with iron replacement and review at three months for symptom response
options.rationale: Combined oral contraceptive pill is a reasonable option but the LNG-IUS has superior bleeding reduction and is the recommended first-line therapy for ovulatory menorrhagia. (Source: RANZCOG: Heavy menstrual bleeding)

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding is defined as bleeding that interferes with physical, emotional or quality of life. Initial workup excludes structural causes (ultrasound) and assesses iron status (FBC, ferritin). The levonorgestrel-releasing IUS is first-line for ovulatory heavy menstrual bleeding without structural pathology, reducing blood loss by around 90 percent at 12 months. Second-line medical options include tranexamic acid during menses, NSAIDs, combined hormonal contraception and oral progestogens. Surgical options (endometrial ablation, hysterectomy) are reserved for failure of medical management. Iron deficiency anaemia is treated concurrently.
key_points: LNG-IUS first-line for ovulatory HMB without structural pathology
Tranexamic acid 1 g TDS during menses reduces loss 30 to 50 percent
Hysteroscopy reserved for intermenstrual bleeding or treatment failure
Treat iron deficiency concurrently
explanation.pearls: An endometrial thickness above 12 mm on ultrasound in a premenopausal woman with HMB warrants hysteroscopy and biopsy to exclude hyperplasia even with a regular cycle.

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 30-year-old para 1 woman 14 days after an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at term. Over the past 24 hours her vaginal loss has changed from light brown to fresh red, soaking through two regular pads per hour. She passed a 50 cent piece sized clot this morning. She has mild lower abdominal cramping but no fever, no foul-smelling discharge and no urinary or bowel symptoms. She has been breastfeeding exclusively and feels otherwise well.

data_table.Examination: Uterus palpable two finger-breadths above the symphysis pubis, mildly tender; speculum shows fresh blood from os, no foreign body; no perineal trauma
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (booking 124), white cell count 9.5, CRP 12; urine pregnancy test positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Request urgent magnetic resonance imaging of the pelvis with gadolinium contrast to characterise the uterine cavity and adjacent soft tissue anatomy
options.rationale: MRI is not the initial imaging modality for secondary PPH; ultrasound is faster, cheaper and adequate to identify retained products as the first-line investigation. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Refer for outpatient hysteroscopy in three weeks to directly visualise the cavity and obtain endometrial biopsy under direct vision
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is reserved for ongoing bleeding after empty ultrasound or for histological confirmation; first-line workup is non-invasive imaging within the same day. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Send high vaginal and endocervical swabs alone and treat empirically for endometritis without imaging before culture results return next week
options.rationale: Endometritis usually presents with fever, foul-smelling discharge and uterine tenderness; afebrile heavy fresh bleeding warrants imaging not empirical antibiotics alone. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Arrange same-day transvaginal pelvic ultrasound to assess the endometrial cavity and exclude retained products of conception causing the secondary bleed
options.rationale: Secondary PPH with a positive urine pregnancy test and bleeding pattern beyond expected lochial timeline warrants ultrasound to identify retained products which is the most common cause. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum haemorrhage)

options.text: Reassure that this is the normal pattern of late lochia rubra and review in seven days only if the bleeding has not settled or fever develops
options.rationale: By day 14 lochia is typically alba (creamy white); a return to fresh red bleeding beyond day 10 is abnormal and warrants investigation, not reassurance. (Source: RANZCOG: Postpartum haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Secondary postpartum haemorrhage is abnormal bleeding from 24 hours to 12 weeks postpartum. The two leading causes are retained products of conception and endometritis (often co-existing). Workup is FBC, group and screen, CRP, endocervical and high vaginal swabs, and pelvic ultrasound. Retained products typically appear as a heterogeneous echogenic cavity mass with vascularity; an empty cavity points to endometritis or atony. Management of retained products is uterotonics plus antibiotic cover and consideration of suction evacuation; management of endometritis is broad-spectrum antibiotics covering anaerobes (intravenous amoxicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole).
key_points: Secondary PPH: 24 hours to 12 weeks postpartum
Ultrasound is first-line imaging to identify retained products
Retained products and endometritis often co-exist
Lochia transition by D14 is alba; fresh red bleeding is abnormal
explanation.pearls: Empirical antibiotic cover for retained products in secondary PPH is intravenous amoxicillin, gentamicin and metronidazole given concurrently with surgical or expectant management decisions.

subtopic_detail: HELLP syndrome diagnosis and delivery planning
stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to see a 34-year-old G2P1 at 33+2 weeks who presents with 12 hours of epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea and three episodes of vomiting. Booking blood pressure was 110/65 and previous antenatal visits have all been unremarkable. Fetal movements are normal. On arrival to the assessment unit her blood pressure has climbed and she now reports a frontal headache. Bloods drawn in triage are returning while you assess her.

data_table.Examination: Epigastric tenderness; brisk reflexes with no clonus; CTG reassuring at 33+2; fundus appropriate for dates
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 78, Hb 110, AST 220, ALT 195, LDH 800, haptoglobin under 0.1, urine protein:creatinine 60 mg/mmol, creatinine 80

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Commence magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis, give betamethasone, control blood pressure with IV labetalol and plan delivery within 48 hours after corticosteroid course
options.rationale: HELLP at 33 weeks warrants magnesium for seizure prophylaxis, antenatal corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, antihypertensive control and delivery within 24 to 48 hours after steroid completion. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy)

options.text: Admit for serial bloods, intravenous fluids, oral nifedipine and conservative observation until 37 weeks gestation to maximise fetal benefit from intrauterine growth
options.rationale: Conservative management beyond 34 weeks gestation in HELLP increases maternal mortality from hepatic rupture, eclampsia and DIC; delivery within 48 hours is indicated after steroids. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy)

options.text: Refer urgently to general surgery for upper abdominal imaging and laparoscopic cholecystectomy on the assumption of biliary colic causing the presentation
options.rationale: Right upper quadrant pain with raised transaminases and thrombocytopenia in late pregnancy is HELLP until proven otherwise; surgery without correcting coagulopathy is dangerous. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange urgent plasma exchange and high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone targeting thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura before any consideration of delivery decision
options.rationale: TTP is a differential but pregnancy-related HELLP is more common; presumptive PLEX is reserved for failure to improve postpartum or ADAMTS13 confirmation, not first step. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy)

options.text: Admit overnight for telemetry monitoring, antiemetics and analgesia, and review the haematology and biochemistry trend on rounds the following morning before escalation
options.rationale: Severe HELLP biochemistry requires the same hour escalation; deferring the management decision until next morning risks maternal seizure, hepatic rupture and fetal compromise overnight. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertensive disorders of pregnancy)

explanation.summary: HELLP syndrome is haemolysis, elevated liver enzymes and low platelets, occurring in around 0.5 percent of pregnancies usually with pre-eclampsia. Diagnostic criteria include platelets under 100, AST or ALT above twice the upper limit of normal and evidence of haemolysis (low haptoglobin, raised LDH, raised bilirubin or schistocytes). Mainstay management is delivery; conservative management beyond 34 weeks is contraindicated. Steroids accelerate fetal lung maturity below 34 weeks and may transiently improve maternal platelets. Magnesium sulfate prevents eclampsia and antihypertensives target blood pressure under 160/110.
key_points: HELLP: haemolysis, elevated transaminases, platelets under 100
Delivery within 24 to 48 hours above 34 weeks gestation
Magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis at diagnosis
BP target under 160/110 with IV labetalol or hydralazine
explanation.pearls: Hepatic capsule haematoma rupture occurs in around 1 percent of HELLP and presents with right upper quadrant pain, shoulder tip pain and shock; urgent CT and laparotomy with hepatic packing are life-saving.

sources.label: Society of Obstetric Medicine of Australia and New Zealand: Pre-eclampsia

stem: You are the gynaecology registrar reviewing a 33-year-old woman in clinic referred for three consecutive first-trimester pregnancy losses at 8, 10 and 9 weeks gestation over the past two years. Her cycles are regular every 28 days. She and her partner are not consanguineous and there is no family history of recurrent miscarriage. She has no symptoms suggestive of thyroid disease, polycystic ovary syndrome or autoimmune disease. She is otherwise well, takes no regular medication and has BMI 24. She and her partner wish to plan a fourth pregnancy.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; thyroid normal; no hirsutism or acanthosis nigricans; bimanual unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.6, prolactin within reference range, HbA1c 5.2 percent; previous pregnancy ultrasounds showed fetal pole with no heartbeat at second-trimester gestation; no products sent for histopathology in prior losses

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial workup?

options.text: Empirically commence low-dose aspirin and low molecular weight heparin from positive pregnancy test in the next conception without any further investigation now
options.rationale: Aspirin and LMWH are reserved for confirmed antiphospholipid syndrome or other indicated thrombophilia; empirical use without diagnosis carries bleeding risk without proven benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Send only a serum endometrial natural killer cell panel and refer for empirical intravenous immunoglobulin treatment in the first trimester of the next pregnancy
options.rationale: NK cell testing and immunoglobulin therapy are not supported by evidence and are not recommended in routine recurrent miscarriage workup; they carry cost and unclear benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Test antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotype, three-dimensional pelvic ultrasound for uterine anomaly and thyroid function before the next conception is attempted
options.rationale: Recommended workup after three consecutive first-trimester losses includes antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotype, uterine cavity assessment and thyroid function as evidence-based tests prior to next conception. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Arrange hysterosalpingogram alone as the sole next investigation and defer all blood tests until imaging has excluded a structural cavity abnormality on radiology
options.rationale: HSG assesses tubal patency primarily; uterine cavity is better assessed by 3D ultrasound or saline-infusion sonography, and blood tests should be done in parallel not deferred. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

options.text: Reassure her that three losses still falls within the population background rate and decline any investigation until she has had a fourth consecutive first-trimester loss
options.rationale: Recurrent miscarriage is defined as three or more consecutive losses; this threshold triggers investigation rather than deferring further losses without workup. (Source: RANZCOG: Recurrent miscarriage)

explanation.summary: Recurrent miscarriage is three or more consecutive first-trimester pregnancy losses. Recommended workup includes antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2 glycoprotein 1) measured 12 weeks apart, parental karyotype to identify balanced translocations, uterine cavity assessment by 3D ultrasound or saline sonography to detect septum or fibroids, and thyroid function. Empirical interventions including immunoglobulin, prednisolone or LMWH without indication are not supported by current evidence. Aspirin plus LMWH is indicated only for confirmed antiphospholipid syndrome. Despite full workup, around 50 percent of recurrent miscarriage cases remain unexplained.
key_points: Workup after 3 consecutive first-trimester losses
Antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotype, uterine cavity, TFTs
Aspirin and LMWH only for confirmed antiphospholipid syndrome
Empirical immunotherapy not supported by evidence
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid antibodies must be confirmed on repeat testing 12 weeks apart to meet diagnostic criteria; a single positive test does not establish the syndrome or justify lifelong therapy.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar at a regional hospital reviewing a 28-year-old G2P1 at 31+4 weeks gestation who presents with two hours of regular painful contractions every five minutes. Speculum confirms a closed cervix on visualisation but transvaginal ultrasound measures cervical length of 18 mm with funnelling. Membranes appear intact, there is no per vaginal bleeding and there are no signs of chorioamnionitis. CTG is reassuring at 31+4 with normal baseline and reactivity. The nearest tertiary neonatal centre with level 3 NICU is 90 minutes away by road.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft between contractions; fundus 32 cm; cephalic presentation; cervix closed on speculum, cervical length 18 mm with funnel on TVUS
data_table.Investigations: FBC and CRP normal; high vaginal swab and GBS sent; urinalysis negative; CTG reassuring

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

options.text: Give betamethasone and arrange immediate caesarean section locally to deliver the infant before progressive cervical change leads to imminent vaginal birth in the unit
options.rationale: Caesarean is not indicated solely for preterm labour with reassuring CTG; vaginal birth is preferred and tocolysis should be used to enable steroid effect and transfer. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm labour)

options.text: Commence indomethacin tocolysis and admit locally for steroids and bedrest with no plan for transfer or for fetal neuroprotection beyond the steroid course
options.rationale: Indomethacin is reserved for under 32 weeks short courses but is not first-line in Australia; magnesium neuroprotection and tertiary transfer are essential under 32 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm labour)

options.text: Allow labour to progress without tocolysis, give betamethasone alone and prepare for vaginal birth locally with neonatal resuscitation team attending the delivery
options.rationale: Tocolysis is warranted under 34 weeks to allow steroid effect and arrange transfer; allowing precipitous birth locally without level 3 NICU risks worse neonatal outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm labour)

options.text: Start oral nifedipine and admit overnight for observation, deferring corticosteroids and magnesium sulfate until the labour pattern is confirmed in 12 hours
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids must be given as soon as preterm birth is anticipated within 7 days; delaying for 12 hours misses the window for full steroid benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm labour)

options.text: Commence nifedipine tocolysis, give betamethasone, administer magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection and transfer to the tertiary neonatal centre in utero
options.rationale: Preterm labour under 32 weeks warrants tocolysis to delay birth for steroid effect, antenatal corticosteroids, magnesium for neuroprotection and in utero transfer to a level 3 NICU. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm labour)

explanation.summary: Preterm labour under 32 weeks is managed with four parallel actions: tocolysis (nifedipine first-line in Australia, atosiban as second-line), antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM two doses 24 hours apart), magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection (4 g loading then 1 g/hour infusion for at least 4 hours and up to 24 hours), and in utero transfer to a level 3 NICU. Magnesium reduces cerebral palsy risk by around 30 percent when given under 32 weeks. Antibiotics are added only for confirmed or suspected GBS positive status or preterm pre-labour rupture of membranes.
key_points: Nifedipine first-line tocolysis in Australia
Betamethasone for fetal lung maturity under 34 weeks
Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection under 32 weeks
In utero transfer to level 3 NICU under 32 weeks
explanation.pearls: Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection ideally commences at least 4 hours before delivery; if delivery occurs within 4 hours, reduced benefit applies but treatment is still worthwhile to start.

subtopic_detail: Early prosthetic valve endocarditis empiric therapy
stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 58-year-old man with two weeks of fever, malaise, anorexia and night sweats. He had a mechanical mitral valve replacement eight months ago for rheumatic mitral regurgitation. On examination he is febrile, looks unwell and has a new pansystolic murmur at the apex that was not documented at his recent valve clinic visit four weeks ago. There are no peripheral stigmata of endocarditis. He has no penicillin allergy. Blood cultures have been drawn from two separate sites and are pending.

data_table.Examination: New pansystolic murmur loudest at apex; clear lungs; no peripheral oedema; no splinter haemorrhages or Janeway lesions on inspection
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14, CRP 180, creatinine 95, lactate 1.4; transthoracic echocardiogram shows 8 mm vegetation on prosthetic mitral valve with mild paravalvular regurgitation
data_table.Medications: Warfarin INR target 2.5 to 3.5; bisoprolol 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin and gentamicin combination tailored to streptococcal and enterococcal native valve coverage with no anti-staphylococcal cover
options.rationale: Native valve regimens do not cover the staphylococcal pathogens prevalent in early prosthetic valve endocarditis; vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin combined with gentamicin and oral rifampicin to cover the staphylococcal pathogens of early prosthetic valve endocarditis
options.rationale: Early prosthetic valve endocarditis (under 12 months from surgery) is most often Staphylococcus epidermidis or aureus; vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin is the empirical regimen. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone monotherapy for streptococcus viridans cover until blood culture sensitivities return to guide narrower targeted treatment
options.rationale: Streptococcus viridans is uncommon in early prosthetic valve endocarditis; staphylococcal coverage with vancomycin plus aminoglycoside plus rifampicin is the empirical standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin monotherapy without combination synergy or rifampicin while awaiting blood culture sensitivities and surgical team discussion next day
options.rationale: Vancomycin monotherapy is inadequate for prosthetic valve endocarditis; combination therapy with aminoglycoside synergy and rifampicin biofilm penetration is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer empirical antibiotics until two further blood cultures and serology have returned to ensure narrowest appropriate spectrum based on confirmed organism identification
options.rationale: Empirical therapy starts immediately after blood cultures drawn; deferring antibiotics in confirmed endocarditis risks valve destruction, embolic phenomena and septic shock. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Early prosthetic valve endocarditis is defined as occurring within 12 months of valve surgery and is typically caused by staphylococcal organisms (Staph epidermidis, Staph aureus) acquired perioperatively. Empirical therapy is intravenous vancomycin plus gentamicin plus oral rifampicin pending blood culture results. Three sets of blood cultures from separate sites are drawn before antibiotics. Transthoracic echocardiogram is the initial imaging; transoesophageal echocardiogram is more sensitive for prosthetic vegetations and paravalvular abscess. Cardiothoracic surgical review is mandatory for prosthetic valve endocarditis to assess surgical indications including heart failure, persistent bacteraemia, large vegetations and paravalvular complications.
key_points: Early PVE under 12 months: empirical vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampicin
Three sets of blood cultures before antibiotics
TOE more sensitive than TTE for prosthetic vegetations
Cardiothoracic surgical review mandatory
explanation.pearls: Surgical indications in PVE include heart failure, paravalvular extension or abscess, persistent bacteraemia after 5 to 7 days appropriate antibiotics, fungal infection and embolic events with persistent vegetation above 10 mm.

sources.label: Cardiac Society of Australia and New Zealand: Infective endocarditis

stem: You are the GP reviewing a 24-year-old non-pregnant woman with 24 hours of fever, right loin pain and dysuria. She has been able to tolerate oral fluids without vomiting and remains haemodynamically stable. She has no past medical history, no allergies, no recent antibiotics and no urological abnormality. She is sexually active and her partner has no symptoms. Urinalysis shows leucocyte esterase strongly positive, nitrites positive and microscopic haematuria. Midstream urine has been sent for culture and sensitivity.

data_table.Examination: Right costovertebral angle tenderness; abdomen otherwise soft; well perfused; chest clear
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis leucocyte++, nitrite+, blood+; serum lactate 1.4; creatinine 70, eGFR above 90; urine pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic plan?

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg once daily for three days as standard short-course empirical treatment for uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection therapy
options.rationale: Three-day trimethoprim is for uncomplicated cystitis; pyelonephritis requires longer course and an agent with better renal tissue penetration. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg twice daily for five days, which is the preferred narrow-spectrum agent for upper urinary tract infection in this clinical setting
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin does not achieve adequate renal tissue concentrations and is contraindicated in pyelonephritis; it is reserved for lower tract infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily for 14 days through ambulatory care intravenous service even in haemodynamically stable patients tolerating oral fluids
options.rationale: Intravenous therapy is reserved for sepsis, vomiting, pregnancy, urological abnormality or failure of oral therapy; oral cefalexin is appropriate here. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 10 days with follow up at 48 hours to confirm clinical and biochemical improvement on therapy
options.rationale: Acute pyelonephritis in a haemodynamically stable patient tolerating oral intake is treated with oral cefalexin for 10 days with 48-hour clinical review; uropathogen susceptibility is usually adequate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Wait for the midstream urine culture sensitivity report before commencing any antibiotic to avoid inappropriate empirical therapy and selection of resistance
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are started immediately after MSU collection in confirmed pyelonephritis to limit renal damage and sepsis progression; sensitivity guides any subsequent narrowing. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Acute pyelonephritis is fever, loin pain and lower urinary tract symptoms with evidence of urinary infection. The most common pathogen is E. coli, followed by Klebsiella, Proteus, Enterococcus and Staph saprophyticus. Empirical oral therapy in non-pregnant, haemodynamically stable patients tolerating oral intake is cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 10 days, or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Intravenous therapy (gentamicin plus ampicillin or ceftriaxone) is reserved for sepsis, vomiting, pregnancy, urological abnormality or failure of oral therapy. Review at 48 hours to confirm clinical improvement and switch to culture-directed therapy.
key_points: Oral cefalexin 500 mg QID for 10 days non-severe pyelonephritis
IV ceftriaxone or gentamicin plus ampicillin for severe or vomiting
Nitrofurantoin and trimethoprim short course only for cystitis
MSU before antibiotics; review at 48 hours
explanation.pearls: An ultrasound or CT kidneys is indicated for pyelonephritis failing to improve within 72 hours, recurrent episodes or any features of obstruction such as oliffuse such as oliguria or worsening loin pain with antibiotic therapy.

sources.label: Kidney Health Australia: Urinary tract infection
